name: Crater of Diamonds State Park
short_description: The only diamond-producing site open to the public in the United States. Visitors can search the eroded volcanic crater for diamonds and other gemstones year-round.
description:
Crater of Diamonds State Park is a 911-acre public park built around a 100-million-year-old volcanic crater in southwestern Arkansas. This is the only place in the United States where the general public can search for diamonds with the guarantee that any diamonds found are theirs to keep. The park's crater was created during the Cretaceous period when a kimberlite pipe brought diamonds and other minerals to the surface. Since the park opened in 1972, visitors have found over 33,000 diamonds, with stones ranging from tiny chips to significant gems weighing several carats.

The diamond-bearing area is approximately 37.5 acres of plowed field that visitors can freely search. The volcanic crater's unique geology creates the perfect environment for diamond discovery. Visitors use simple tools like screens, shovels, and picks to sift through the weathered volcanic material. The park provides educational displays explaining the geological processes that brought diamonds to this remote Arkansas location.

While diamonds are the main attraction, the crater also produces other gemstones including amethyst, quartz, calcite, and barite. Most diamonds found here are industrial-grade stones, though gem-quality diamonds are occasionally discovered. The park's gentle slope and accessible terrain make it suitable for families and people of various skill levels. Picnic areas, restrooms, and a visitor center with exhibits support extended visits.

The experience at Crater of Diamonds differs from typical rockhounding sites because you are actively searching through prepared ground rather than working natural outcrops. The park maintains the search area by periodically plowing fields to bring fresh material to the surface. This systematic approach increases the chances of finding diamonds compared to looking in untouched geological formations elsewhere.
history: The Crater of Diamonds formed approximately 100 million years ago during the Cretaceous period when a kimberlite pipe erupted, bringing diamond-bearing material from the Earth's mantle to the surface. This volcanic activity created the distinctive crater structure visible today, making it one of the few diamond sources in North America. The site remained a local curiosity until it was developed as a state park in 1972, transforming it into the world's only public diamond-mining operation where visitors retain ownership of their finds.
tips: Search the plowed fields methodically, focusing on areas where diamonds have been found recently, as the park's maintenance crew regularly turns over fresh material. Bring a screen or classifier to sift through volcanic soil efficiently, and look for the glassy luster and angular crystal forms that distinguish diamonds from quartz. Work the ground after recent rains when the soil is moist and easier to break apart, increasing your chances of spotting stones that would otherwise blend with dry material. Since most diamonds here are small industrial stones, examine your soil carefully and don't overlook tiny crystals that could be legitimate diamonds.
terrain_notes: The site sits at 377 feet elevation on a gentle slope within a volcanic crater basin. The search area consists of approximately 37.5 acres of regularly plowed open field with minimal vegetation. Walking distance from parking to the main search area is typically under a quarter mile. The footing is uneven due to freshly turned soil and volcanic rock fragments, requiring sturdy footwear but accessible to most visitors without climbing or scrambling.
best_season: Year-round, though October through April offers the most comfortable conditions. Spring and fall temperatures are moderate, making extended fieldwork enjoyable. Summer heat can be intense on the open field with limited shade, while winter months are mild for Arkansas. Visit after recent rainfall when soil is workable; avoid peak heat of July and August.
written_directions: From Murfreesboro, take Arkansas Highway 301 south approximately 2 miles to the state park entrance. Follow the main park road to the visitor center and parking area. The park is well-marked with signs along the highway. The final approach is on paved roads suitable for all passenger vehicles.
road_conditions: Paved roads to parking area. The search field consists of plowed soil with volcanic fragments.
parking_notes: Large designated parking lot near visitor center with ample space. Parking is included with admission. Accessible parking available near facilities.
collection_rules: Diamonds and gemstones found are the property of the finder and may be kept. No commercial mining or selling of collected stones is permitted. Collection is limited to the designated search area maintained by the park. Visitors may not use motorized equipment or excavate beyond the top few inches of soil. No collecting outside the designated diamond search field.
quantity_limits: Keep what you find - no weight limit. Visitors retain full ownership of all gemstones discovered.
rules:
Search only in designated plowed areas
Keep all gemstones you find
No motorized equipment allowed
No commercial collection
No excavation beyond plowed surface
Follow park hours and rules posted at entrance
nearest_services: Murfreesboro, approximately 2 miles south. Gas stations, restaurants, and lodging available in town.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain on open field with gentle slopes accessible to most visitors. Uneven plowed soil requires sturdy footwear but no climbing or scrambling. Visitor center and restrooms are fully accessible. Parking near facilities available for those with mobility limitations. Wheelchair users may have difficulty navigating the plowed field itself.

faq.question: Can you really find diamonds at Crater of Diamonds State Park in Arkansas?
faq.answer: Yes, over 33,000 diamonds have been found at Crater of Diamonds since it opened as a public park in 1972. Most are small industrial-grade stones, but gem-quality diamonds are occasionally discovered. You keep any diamonds you find, making it the only public diamond-mining site in the United States where visitors retain ownership of their discoveries.

faq.question: How much does it cost to rockhound for diamonds at Crater of Diamonds?
faq.answer: Crater of Diamonds charges a daily admission fee to access the search fields. Rates vary by season and vehicle type, typically ranging from $8-15 per vehicle. The fee includes access to the 37.5-acre plowed diamond search area, visitor center exhibits, and park facilities. Children may have discounted rates. Check the Arkansas State Parks website for current pricing.

faq.question: What tools do I need to find diamonds at Crater of Diamonds State Park?
faq.answer: A screen or classifier is essential for sifting volcanic soil efficiently. Bring a small shovel or spade, a pick to break up hardened soil, and a sturdy digging tool. A magnifying glass helps identify small stones. Wear gloves to protect your hands from sharp volcanic rock fragments. The park allows visitors to bring their own tools, and basic equipment is sometimes available for purchase or rent.

faq.question: What is the best time to rockhound for diamonds in Murfreesboro, Arkansas?
faq.answer: October through April offers the most comfortable conditions for extended searching. Spring and fall temperatures are moderate, making fieldwork enjoyable. Summer heat on the open field can be intense with limited shade. Visit after recent rainfall when the plowed soil is moist and easier to work through, as diamonds are more visible and the material breaks apart more readily.

faq.question: Can families and children go diamond hunting at Crater of Diamonds State Park?
faq.answer: Yes, Crater of Diamonds is highly family-friendly and beginner-suitable for children ages 6 and up. The flat, open terrain and simple tools required make it accessible to young rockhounders. The park offers educational exhibits and facilities including picnic areas and restrooms. Children often have success finding small diamonds and other gemstones, making it an exciting first rockhounding experience.

faq.question: Are there other gemstones besides diamonds at Crater of Diamonds State Park?
faq.answer: Yes, while diamonds are the primary attraction, the volcanic crater also produces amethyst, quartz, calcite, and barite. These secondary gemstones are sometimes found alongside diamonds in the search field. The amethyst and quartz specimens can be attractive collector pieces, adding variety to your discoveries beyond the excitement of finding diamonds.

meta_title: Crater of Diamonds State Park - Rockhounding in Arkansas
meta_description: Find diamonds at Crater of Diamonds State Park near Murfreesboro, Arkansas. Public diamond hunting with fee access. Keep all gems you find.
